data.title: Shell: Refocusing Will Take Time (Probably A Few Years)
data.description:
My last article on Shell plc (SHEL) discussed the refocusing effort. Management summarized the effort with the following chart:

As shown above, this effort is going to take some time. Larger companies that are the size of a supermajor often begin a project several years ahead of when it becomes apparent to shareholders.

In this case, what is shown above should become apparent to shareholders shortly. Managements with goals like this often have ways to measure their success. Many times, that measurement of success is presented to shareholders. Shareholders can then judge the effectiveness of management. For investors, that should mean that the corporate breakeven will be slowly declining.

More importantly, a management like this is highly likely to replace achieved goals with more goals to "keep the ball rolling." After all, technology does not only improve operations, but it also improves the office as well. That "structural cost reductions" is likely to be both in the office and in operations.

Many are very aware that operations throughout the industry have experienced some material improvements for quite some time. Office improvements are a bit less visible because the "office costs" are often much smaller than the operating costs. But it is every bit as important to keep staff and overhead "lean and mean."

Therefore, shareholders should expect management to find tangible measurements that demonstrate progress until that progress becomes obvious in the form of increasing profitability (that will at least partially offset inflation).

Iranian Situation

This situation has impacted the supermajors. So far, the negatives of the situation seem to be outweighed by the positives. While I sure hope it continues that way, there is no guarantee that will be the case.

When I think of Shell, generally what is shown above is what I usually focus upon.

However, there is more:

This company has a bit more in the diversified part of the company than is the case with other super majors. Note that I did not include the guidance on the tax effect (for example) among the minor issues because I do not consider that as significant as what is shown.

The thing to note is that earnings are expected to be significantly higher than was the case for the first quarter of 2025. That seems to be true pretty much throughout the industry.

The reason would appear to be the magnitude of the increase in commodity prices has far outweighed both the losses from the situation and the short period of time that commodity prices increased.

What is becoming apparent to the market is that the small losses across the industry are becoming material enough to cause shortages. Now, should these shortages become predictable enough (and last long enough), then there may be production that comes online to replace the missing production (and hence bring commodity prices back down).

One Risk

There is a sizable risk that repairs are completed when demand and supply come back into line (or shortly after that). The repairs that the market anticipates could then come online to cause a cyclical downturn from a surplus of supplies of various products.

Possible Strategy

One possible strategy is to invest in the industry in accordance with the risk that each investor can handle. When the shortage is projected to disappear, it would likely be time to sell for an intermediate-term trade idea.

The reasoning behind this is that this situation was never sold to the American public. Therefore, all the usual backing from the average person has not occurred. Along with that has been a very sluggish response by the market to the situation.

What may happen here is a better stock price response because the time period to fix the items that need fixing is more certain and therefore much easier for the market to value. The result could well be higher stock prices throughout the industry.

Some may well like to keep a core position as well. Since the current market reply to rising commodity prices has been less than is usually perceived to be the case, it is very possible that a decent long-term profit can be made from the current price. The downside is protected by the efficiency drive shown earlier in the article.

Stock Price

The stock price has "been on a tear" all year, as the chart below shows:

The key here to this idea is that the price-earnings ratio (even though it's forward) has been largely based upon a current view of prices that did not include a major disruption. We are now apparently coming around to the idea that commodity prices will remain elevated for a while. Though that "while" is still indeterminate, a minimum "while" appears to be coming into focus that will enable higher stock prices in the not-too-distant future (famous last words in this notoriously low-visibility industry).

As such, that price-earnings ratio is already cheap in a fairly expensive market. Given the fact that management is expecting higher first-quarter earnings, it is becoming more reasonable to expect commodity prices to be elevated the whole fiscal year.

So far, the industry is not responding to those higher prices because no one knows how to plan the payback periods that would justify more capital. Instead, right now "everyone" is treating this as a one-time bonanza. But that will only make the high prices last longer.

Seeking Alpha Quant System

Seeking Alpha shows the following long-term history:

This picture may be one of the root causes of moving the company headquarters to England as well as the drive to increase efficiency shown above.

Now if both moves are successful, then some quant system measurements will change in the future:

The current Seeking Alpha Quant System is historically based. So, the "Factor Grades" shown above reflect that lackluster stock price history. But I believe what management has done is put some programs in place that should result in a better Growth Factor Grade, which will likely affect the Momentum Grade as well.

Revisions will likely get an immediate boost from the Iranian situation that led to elevated commodity prices. That allows for more time for the other improvements.

The slowest improvement will likely be the valuation situation. Right now, that valuation reflects the fact that the stock is out of favor. This is the one grade that will likely decline as the stock price becomes more valued compared to its peers due to the efficiency drive that is under-way.

Summary

Shell is a stock that has been out of favor for good reason for quite some time. However, management would appear to be finally motivated to do something about the situation. As such, the current moves to improve performance provide considerable downside protection for when the day comes that commodity prices inevitably begin the downward part of the cycle.

For a supermajor, this remains a strong buy idea. Understand that large companies generally do not have the appreciation potential of smaller companies. But they are much more diversified to resist the worst parts of the business cycle than their smaller brethren.

When the dividend is included, a total return in the teens would appear to be a minimum expectation plus some price-earnings ratio expansion from the current levels. Since the price-earnings ratio expansion is a longer-term prospect that relies upon an improving performance record, that improvement could rely a whole lot on the stock market atmosphere "when we get there."

It does seem like that price-earnings ratio should be at least 12 today rather than what is shown above. But let us see what happens in the future.

Risks

In addition to the risk discussed above, the whole performance project improvement drive could fail to produce tangible results or the anticipated benefits. That would keep future returns low and eliminate any potential future downside protection.

Performance improvement drives can also be aborted too soon to produce proper results as well.

Future commodity prices are both volatile and low visibility. A severe and sustained downturn may well cause a complete re-evaluation of the current situation.

Supermajors tend to have a reserve replacement issue. They also tend to take care of that issue by making suitable acquisitions. This, of course, depends upon there being suitable acquisitions available. If there are none, then reserves could decline for a period of time. It is also possible that acquired reserves fail to meet the expectations of management.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Toyota Motor: Expected Positive Results, But Headwinds Ahead - Earnings Preview
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I rate Toyota Motors (TM) with a Hold. The company still remains one of the best performers among the Automotive players, having increased its revenues for four years straight and holding one of the highest Operating margin in the industry.

I expect positive news from the next Q4 earnings release (scheduled for May 8, 2026), with the management that has always been conservative in the guidance for the full-year results and the company still holding some pricing power in Japan and in critical regions such as North America.

Nonetheless, my blended DCF-Trading multiples valuation returned an implied share price of $166.97, a ~14% discount to the current levels. Moreover, headwinds from the Middle East situation, the tariffs persistence, and the macroeconomic uncertainty make me more cautious in positively judging the stock.

I advise investors owning shares to hold them until the next earnings release, while new investors should wait for the financial effects of the political and economic drivers mentioned above, investing in the stock only if it goes below the $165-170 threshold.

Business Overview

Toyota Motor Corporation (NYSE:TM) is the largest OEM in the Automotive sector by units of cars produced (with 9,362 thousands vehicles produced and sold) and the second largest by revenue generated ($320.2B of revenues in FY2025).

As reported by the company’s last annual report, Toyota has a presence around the world with six main segments, in which it carries out both manufacturing and distribution activities. These include Japan, North America, Europe, Asia, Central & LATM, and Others. For those who are unfamiliar with the Automotive brands, Toyota, in addition to its historical mass-market brand (including vehicles like Corolla, Aygo, Yaris, RAV4…), is responsible for the production and the selling of the Lexus brand, considered the premium/luxury solution.

The primary business model is, of course, centered around the assembly and the commercialization of cars, pickups, and SUVs, with 170,000 people employed in 168 different markets, but the company also has the financial services business (TFS), focused on lease origination mainly for the consumer and retail customer segment. This business contributes to the financial statements of the company through $300B of receivables and $4.09B of operating income generated.

Toyota generated 27% of the revenues in Japan, but the same geographical area contributed to 53% of the first 9M of FY2026. On the other hand, North America holds the first place in terms of volume of cars generated (1,991 thousands of units in FY2025), but, at the same time, it contributes the lowest margin among the regional segments. This was inevitably due to the different US tariffs introduced at the beginning of 2025 that targeted all the vehicles built in Asia. The impact on the Operating income of the first 9 months of FY2026 was huge, with -$0.04B, down from the $3.50B generated in FY2025.

Management’s Goals Evaluation

Before going on to the analysis of the latest financial results and the earnings preview, I would like to have a look at the stated management’s goals for FY2026. These can be articulated in four pillars:

  • Reinforce the manufacturing activities and reduce the break-even volume.
  • Deepen the supplier base through key productivity initiatives.
  • Widen the company’s value chain, which includes TFS, accessories, second-hand cars, and connected services.
  • Protect the Japanese manufacturing activities instead of relocating the production after the tariffs impact.

Starting with the first goal, Toyota has longstanding benefits from the Toyota Production System, which has always enabled the company to generate higher profitability than its peers. This profitability has remained relatively high also in FY2025 and FY2026, demonstrating the company’s ability to reduce the break-even from operations. On the other hand, I have to signal the rising labor, energy, and raw-materials costs (due to the economic and political instability) that affected Toyota’s gains. Overall, small changes have been made, and the impact on the margins was more defensive than improving.

For the second goal, the supplier network remained stable, with the company that has never suffered from disruptions in the supply chain and in the production processes, but the impacts on the earnings are limited.

The third pillar can be considered the most successful one, with TFS remaining the main contributor to profit generation thanks to rising interest rates, while all the after-sales activities and the used-car business provided the company with recurring revenues at a quite high margin.

Also, the last goal has been positively achieved by the company, with Toyota, which remained with a strong manufacturing presence in Japan, also helped by a weaker yen that facilitated the investments. Obviously, the impact on the short-term profitability of the company cannot be considered positive, with tariffs hitting harder, but I am confident the strategic effects on the long-term production strength of the company will be positive, with the preservation of the manufacturing capacity and the supply chain relationships.

Financial Results

Before diving into what I am expecting from the company’s Q4 2026 results, expected to come out May 8, 2026, I would like to look at the most recent financial performances of the company. The table below summarizes the main financial metrics from FY2021 to FY2025:

Revenues constantly increased for the whole four-year period, registering a 76% cumulative gain; more importantly, the company’s Operating income rose faster, from $14.7B to $32.0B, but not linearly, with a reduction caused by the disruptions in the supply chain that affected industries such as the semiconductor one. The recovery for Toyota started immediately after in FY2024, with margins up again to 11.87%, thanks also to a weaker yen and strategic price actions by the company, which shows good resilience compared to other peers in the Automotive sector.

In terms of vehicle unit sales, Figure 2 above shows a constant increase until FY2024, with a slight reduction in FY2025. At the same time, the revenue per unit sold spiked considerably from $23.7k to $34.2k, again, thanks to the structural weakness of the yen compared to the dollar.

In terms of profitability, Toyota’s ROE reached the highest peak in FY2024 (14.5%) before stabilizing at ~13% in FY2025. The reduction was mainly due to an expansion in the denominator, the book value of the company, which increased after retained earnings exceeded the buybacks, and the overall effect was higher than the earnings increase in the numerator. On the other hand, the ROA of 5.1% is structurally lower than the industry median of roughly 8% because of the considerable impact of TFS business on the company’s balance sheet through the high amount of receivables described earlier in the article.

In terms of geographic performance, Japan is the main contributor to the company’s Operating income, followed by Asia and Europe. Interestingly, looking at the compared performances of FY2025 versus FY2026 9M, it can be noticed how North America’s (and so the U.S.'s) contribution to Operating income was already declining before the whole tariffs impact (from $3.50B in FY2024 to $0.70B in FY2025 and -$0.04B in 9M FY2026).

Focusing more on the 9M FY2026 performances, so far, Toyota showed a YoY increase in revenues of 6.8% to $253.9B, but with a declining Operating income of -13.1%. The management explained the main drivers of these financial results in the last Q3 release: first of all, the company’s efforts in cost-reduction programs were offset by headwinds in raw materials costs; marketing efforts contributed positively with a $4.97B increase thanks to higher pricing power, while an increase in expenses had a negative impact of -$9.77B, mainly driven by the tariffs effect. Finally, other drivers like the Hino reversal and the inflation effects accounted for an increase of $3.68B.

The management guidance for Q4 FY2026 was positive, with an expected increase in revenues of $79.4B and a full-year revenue guidance of $333.3B. Operating margin is expected to come in at 7.6% for the fiscal year, with a decline of 80 bps from FY2025, but still higher than what the consensus was expecting when tariffs were first introduced last year.

Talking about shareholder’s returns, the forecasted dividend of $0.63 per share was confirmed in the last quarterly presentation.

Earnings Preview

As reported in the previous paragraph, the management’s guidance for Q4 FY2026 involves expected revenues of $74.9B, together with $4.02B of additional Operating income and $3.59B in Net income, with EPS standing at $2.76.

Looking at Toyota’s history, Q4 has almost always been the strongest quarter of the year in terms of revenues, but the guidance given in the last earnings release implies the lowest Operating margin among the whole four quarters. I think this is driven by the remaining $1.67B tariff effect, expected by the management in this last quarter, combined with strategic marketing discounts finalized at emptying the company’s inventory in North America.

In addition to the company’s guidance scenario, I computed two alternative scenarios:

  • The “Tariff/FX bite extends” is the pessimistic one, where performances go worse than what the management is expecting, with higher tariffs impact on Operating and Net income and EPS of $1.62;
  • The “Conservative-guide beat” is the bullish one, in which Toyota performs better than expected with a surprisingly higher pricing power, especially in North America, influencing Sales, Operating income and the bottom line, translating directly into higher EPS.

I am personally expecting slightly positive results from the next earnings release, with Operating profit standing between $25.5B and $26.0B for the full year and Q4 EPS of $2.85-3.03. Moreover, historically, Toyota’s management has always been conservative in the full year’s guidance, with FY2025 also beating the company’s expectations.

These assumptions that I have just made directly translate into the assumptions I will use in my DCF to value the stock in the next paragraph.

Valuation

I decided to use a sum-of-parts DCF (valuing the industrial business of the company and adding the TFS business) and a relative valuation using trading multiples to derive an implied share price for Toyota.

DCF

Starting with the fundamental valuation, I estimate Toyota’s cost of capital using Damodaran’s approach and data.

For the cost of equity, I relied on the CAPM formula, using the 4-year risk-free rate in USD (matching as much as possible the duration of the estimated cash flows), the levered beta of the company, and a weighted equity risk premium that takes into consideration the geographical distribution of the company’s revenue.

For the cost of debt, I considered only the interest-bearing debt of the industrial business and the pre-tax cost of debt of 4.50%, which is the company’s USD equivalent yield for the senior unsecured debt. The WACC is estimated as follows:

As already mentioned above, I used the full-year estimated scenario for the next earnings release as a base to estimate the cash flows. I then assumed industrial sales growth of 3% each year, together with a recovery in operating margins towards 8.5% in FY2028, giving credibility to the company’s improvement in the profit structure and a partial declining effect of tariffs. CapEx was modeled at roughly $12-13B per year, following the FY2024-FY2025 trends, with D&A running at $8-10B per year. I assumed a terminal growth rate of 2% to estimate the terminal value of the business.

The main assumptions and the estimated cash flows are summarized in the following table:

The estimated enterprise value for the industrial business is $212.31B. Adding then the TFS segment, with equity of $50B and cash of $52.79B, and subtracting industrial debt of $33.34B and minority interests of $6.79B, I arrived at an estimated share price of $211.07, a 9.74% premium to the current share price of ~$192.

To illustrate how the share price can change with changes in WACC and in the terminal growth rate, I integrated the DCF with a sensitivity table:

Relative Valuation

I decided to benchmark Toyota against a peer group of similar global players in the automotive business, composed of Honda, Volkswagen, Ford, GM, Stellantis, and Nissan. The comparison has been made on three key multiples: P/E, P/BV, and EV/EBITDA, taken from Yahoo Finance and Seeking Alpha.

The picture below shows the results of the analysis:

I then applied interquartile ranges to the estimated multiples, deriving the share prices:

The relative method implies that Toyota is currently priced at a 50% premium versus the median multiples, which is double the premium that the company has usually commanded against the peer group (around 25%).

Football Field

Taking together the intrinsic valuation approach and the pricing method of multiples, I arrived at an estimated share price of $166.97, as shown in the football field below.

The blended share price is roughly 14% below the current share price, with the sum-of-parts DCF being the only valuation method that supports a higher share price. This result, however, relies a lot on Toyota’s ability to recover towards an 8.5% Operating margin by FY2028. This scenario is obviously possible but non-trivial after the Iran war started last February, which will have an impact on the company’s ability to generate profitability.

Limits and Risks

Among all the different risks and factors that an investor has to take into account, there is the impact of the war in Iran. The intensity of such impact still has to be seen in the company’s financials, probably starting with Q1 of the next fiscal year.

I tried to run some numbers to quantify it and see if the market has already correctly priced it in the stock price:

  • S&P Global Mobility reported a reduction in US 2026 MHCV demand by 1%, a number that is expected to increase with the next forecast due in May. I believe the overall demand impact can be estimated at around 5-8% if we take into account the elasticity of the Automotive industry to declines in the overall GDP and inflation effects
  • Middle East makes up roughly 2% of Toyota’s global sales
  • I am expecting a rise due to inflation pressure in key materials such as steel and aluminum, plus a general increase in logistic costs, with an estimated supply chain impact lower than the one that happened in the semiconductor shortage (around 10% on average) but still significant at around 2-4%
  • The stock declined approximately 2% in single escalation days and between 8-12% in the overall war period

Taking everything into consideration, I expect a decline in the EPS of around 8-10% starting from Q1 FY2027.

Combined with Iran’s war, there is obviously the risk tied to the persistence of the tariff regime, which will likely continue in FY2027 if the US policy remains unchanged and the company cannot pass this cost to final consumers. About this topic, there is evidence from the Q3 FY2026 release of a declining pricing power of the company, which might negatively affect the share price through compressions in the Operating margin.

Not only, does macroeconomic uncertainty come from the Yen/Dollar exchange rate, but quantitative tightening from the BOJ or rate declines from the FED would also have negative effects on the company’s revenue.

Finally, any possible de-rating of the already-weak automotive industry would more negatively affect the companies with the highest multiples in the sector, and, as we saw above, Toyota is one of those.

Conclusion

Toyota Motor remains one of the most qualitative automotive brands in the world, with Operating margin higher than other US brands such as Ford and GM and an abnormal ability to absorb US tariffs.

I expect slightly positive results from the next earnings release in May, but future headwinds from the Iran war and the whole macroeconomic uncertainty are not only possible but probable. Also, the blended valuation performed implies a share price 14% lower than the current one.

Taking everything into consideration, I rate Toyota as a hold, waiting for the earnings expected May 8. I advise investors already owning the stock to wait until the Q4 results, given the historically conservative management guidance for the last quarter. New investors should either wait and see the financial effects of the ongoing Middle East war or buy the stock if it goes down towards the $165-170 levels.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Renesas Electronics: Q1 Outperformance And Guidance Miss Draw Attention
data.description:
I've stayed "Neutral" on Renesas Electronics Corporation (RNECF) (RNECY) (6723.T).

The 1Q2026 EBITDA beat and below-expectations 2Q2026 operating margin present a mixed picture, justifying a “Hold.”

My July 29, 2025, initiation piece outlined both the encouraging and unfavorable read-throughs from RNECF's mid-year investor event and 2Q25 earnings call.

Q1 Profit Grew At A Faster Pace

The firm's latest financials were disclosed on April 24.

RNECF recorded an EBITDA of ¥146 billion for Jan-Mar '26. This topped the sell-side's projection by 7.4%, according to S&P Capital IQ. The implied YoY expansion was +41% versus +30% in the preceding quarter.

In my view, the positive surprise was achieved with solid automotive-related demand, margin-accretive portfolio shifts, and scale economies.

I highlighted RNECF's “market-leading core auto MCU (microcontrollers) operations” in my write-up last year. That division proved to be a standout as its YoY sales increase improved from 4Q25's +9.8% to 1Q26's +10.8%.

The group explained at the analyst call that its new “generation 4 SoC R-Car is ramping up very successfully.” Management also added that the more mature solutions “are also showing strong growth.”

There was an optimal change in the contribution of RNECF's products with varying “gross profit margins” or “GPMs” too.

The actual 1Q2026 sales of low-GPM digital power solutions were lower than anticipated due to supply-side issues. On the flip side, its high-GPM applications pertaining to “AI Data Centers/AIDC” were highly sought-after during the same timeframe.

RNECF's companywide GP-to-revenue widened by 250 bps YoY to 59.2% for the first quarter this year.

Furthermore, the enterprise's fab loading level went up 6 ppts sequentially to 55% in 1Q26. I think the more substantial overhead absorption should have boosted its EBITDA.

It's worth mentioning the -3.4% and -4.7% price declines for the RNECF and RNECY tickers last Friday post-earnings. My take is that investors were worried about the company's near-term prospects, notwithstanding its Q1 outperformance. I will address this point in the next section.

Anticipating Weaker Profitability

RNECF has guided for a 2Q2026 EBIT-to-sales of 29.0%. That's lower than the 1Q26 and FY25 figures of 33.7% and 29.5%, respectively. The prior consensus estimate was also a relatively superior 30.7%.

At the results briefing, management mainly attributed this expected margin deterioration to “utility cost increases.” I fear that this might not be a one-off. SA News' recent report indicated that “oil prices moved higher on Monday” as “limited shipments through the Strait of Hormuz continued to tighten global crude supplies.”

The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (GS) has also lifted “its fourth-quarter oil price forecasts to $90 per barrel for Brent crude” today. That's consistent with RNECF's briefing comments that “we do not expect gross margin to continue to rise,” considering “the current crude oil situation.”

As per S&P Capital IQ consensus, analysts are now still predicting a 60 bps wider GPM for full-year FY2026. I see its shares taking another beating if there are further profitability-related forecast downgrades.

Pricing Signals Are Murky

RNECF's CEO didn't give a direct response at the results meeting when asked about the possibility of charging more for the firm's products. On one hand, the group noted that “rising costs and supply constraints” point to a likely upward shift in prices at “some point in time.” But it also acknowledged that “no customer would like to see that” happen.

In my opinion, RNECF will most probably leave things unchanged for now.

I think the extended automotive chip development cycles give suppliers the upper hand in negotiating with OEMs, as switching is too costly. But the company is lagging its rivals in terms of EV transitions, so it might not want to get priced out and cede market share. Its earnings call remarks indicated that “our growth rate would be muted” versus peers “with an (industry) shift to BEV (Battery Electric Vehicle).”

The Good And The Bad About Capital Investment Surge

RNECF's CAPEX-to-topline jumped from below 10% in the past few quarters to 30+% in 1Q26, as per the chart presented below.

Historical CAPEX

The firm is spending significantly more money to scale up.

I like how this initiative supports RNECF's goal of targeting high-growth segments and bringing more of its production in-house. Management is expecting the revenue for its “AI businesses” to expand by +100% in FY2026, validating their potential. Moreover, part of the cash outlay is intended for increasing the size of its Japanese manufacturing bases.

But the additional capacity will only come online two years from now. In the interim, RNECF is still supply-constrained and unable to fully capitalize on customer demand. To make things worse, its margins could trend lower in FY2028 and beyond when the associated depreciation and maintenance expenses start hitting the P&L.

My Conclusion

I'm still rating this name as “Hold.” There's too much uncertainty about RNECF's future performance for me to be outright bullish or bearish.

The stock is reasonably valued based on my benchmarking exercise.

The consensus forward EV/EBITDA for RNECF, NXP Semiconductors N.V. (NXPI), and STMicroelectronics N.V. (STM) is 12.4x. 13.5x and 13.6x, respectively, as per S&P Capital IQ. They are all at a similar low-teens level.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: MSTU: Leveraged ETFs Are Interesting (But Stay Away From This One)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I recommend investors stay away from the T-REX 2X Long MSTR Daily Target (BATS:MSTU) ETF. My intention in this article is to answer a question from a follower who is intrigued by the recent rise in Bitcoin and asked me what I thought of Strategy's leveraged ETFs.

What is the Best (or Worst) Leveraged ETF for This Topic?

Before starting the reasoning, the question caught my attention, and I looked for the main leveraged ETFs for the objective, and they are below. Initially MSTU seems the most interesting, as it gives 2x leverage to the theme with the highest liquidity ($574 million in AUM) and lowest expense ratio (1.05%), which is why I will focus the analysis on this ETF.

Manager Tuttle Capital delivers 2x the daily performance of Strategy (NASDAQ:MSTR) through swaps and derivatives. This option is not ideal for you to buy and expect 2x the performance of Strategy shares in the medium and long term (weeks, months, or years). This ETF should be used by bold investors (usually day traders) who want short-term exposure to the thesis.

Context

I'm not sure if the question originated from my investment theses in Bitcoin and in leveraged ETFs on other themes; maybe not. I will preserve the identity of what follows, but I confess that I found the topic relevant to also explain the strategy of Michael Saylor's company.

What Does Strategy Do?

The company is a Bitcoin treasury and has 815,000 bitcoins, almost 4% of the total bitcoins that will be produced. The market believes that Michael Saylor's company could reach 1 million Bitcoins this year due to its aggressive purchases.

If you're not familiar, almost every Monday Michael Saylor posts that he purchased a certain number of Bitcoins. Strategy is by far the company with the most Bitcoins in the world.

But how does the company finance all these purchases? There are two ways!

The first is through the sale of MSTR common shares on the market, and the second is the issuance of preferred shares called STRC (NASDAQ:STRC) that distributes dividends.

As stated, Strategy's average Bitcoin price is $75,527, so if Bitcoin falls below this price, the company will make a loss.

For those who don't know, the company also has STRC shares, which pay dividends of 11.5% per year (and increasing).

This is relevant because regardless of whether the price of Bitcoin rises or falls, the company will have this obligation to pay dividends to investors. The STRC is fundamental to understanding the thesis.

How Does STRC Work?

Since it was created, the dividend has only been increasing. This is because the STRC was created to always trade close to $100 as its face value, similar to a bond. When the share price drops below this, the company increases its dividend to attract new investors, which causes the share to rise and return to close to $100. The following graph shows how this strategy has worked over time.

On the other hand, if at any point the STRC rises above $100, the company may reduce dividends (because Strategy will not need to attract the investor's attention). Therefore, the less attractive the crypto market is, the more expensive it is to keep STRC trading close to $100.

Understanding the Strategy in Depth

Strategy raised more than $7 billion to buy Bitcoin through this STRC engineering last year. In 2026, the company intends to make its preferred stocks more compelling.

But if the price of Bitcoin remains low for a long time, the company may not have the strength to keep the STRC close to $100, which could reduce financing for Bitcoin purchases.

Now let's get to the numbers! If the company has $7 billion in issued STRC shares and obligations to pay 11.50% in dividends, there is an annual payment obligation around $805 million. But Bitcoin doesn't generate dividends; where will the capital come from if the market turns sour?

The company has a cash reserve of $1.4 billion, so the cash would cover just over a year and a half of operations. But if the winter is longer, there is a problem, as the issuance of new shares may not attract investors, and then the company would have to sell Bitcoins.

The Domino Effect of Bitcoin Sales

Of course, this is an extreme scenario, but it can happen! Therefore, let's imagine a scenario in which Bitcoin falls below $40,000 (a hypothesis raised by Saylor) for a period longer than a year.

In this case, the m/NAV plummets, and the company will not be able to issue new shares. In parallel, STRC would trade below $100 frequently, which would require increasing the dividend and would drain cash quickly. In this scenario, cash reserves would probably not last a year, and the company would have to sell its Bitcoins at some point.

This could generate a dangerous domino effect; after all, the sale of Bitcoins would put pressure on the price of Bitcoin, which worsens the company's situation. In parallel, the market will see the desperate company itself selling its Bitcoins and may sell along.

My Verdict

If used intelligently, leveraged ETFs are excellent, but in this case, did you realize how extremely complex this Strategy leverage engineering is? So why expose yourself to this leveraged thesis? Therefore, to answer the follower's question, I do not recommend buying the T-REX 2X Long MSTR Daily Target ETF due to the complexity of the thesis and leverage.

Risks

The scenario I discussed is Strategy's nightmare; however, the company can take several measures to prevent the worst-case scenario. Before this extreme, they could very well abandon the face value strategy or also reduce dividends to maintain operations for a longer time.

Not to mention that when the m/NAV drops below 1, investors can see this as an opportunity and buy, which also holds the price down. Finally, perhaps the biggest risk mitigator is that the majority of Strategy's debt is made up of bonds that can be converted into MSTR shares upon maturity. This means they do not require cash payment and can be converted directly into shares. The first large batch of this debt only matures in 2028 and the next in 2029.

The Bottom Line

I reiterate the statement that I find leveraged ETFs quite interesting. However, they are interesting when you need exposure to an asset with low volatility or when you believe there is a short window for an investment and you want to amplify the return.

However, neither of the two reasons above applies in the current case. What it seems is that Bitcoin should rise again with low volatility, not to mention that the company's strategy is extremely complex, and there is no need to amplify this complexity with a leveraged ETF.

Based on this analysis, I recommend that investors stay away from MSTU. The risk-return ratio is definitely not attractive here.

data.title: Meta's Stock May Plunge Following Results
data.description:
Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) has surged nearly 30% since late March. Now, the moment of truth comes. When the company reports results on April 29, the risk isn't whether the results are good or bad; it is that the stock may give back the bulk of those gains, if not all, regardless.

While the company is expected to report strong revenue growth in the first quarter, up 31.1% y/y to $55.5 billion, earnings growth is expected to be muted, rising just 5.5% y/y to $6.78 per share. For the second quarter, the company is expected to guide revenue to $59.4 billion, a growth rate of 25.1%.

Of course, the market has become more concerned about Meta's rising operating expenses and CapEx. Both of which are expected to show significant growth in 2026. Operating expenses are expected to rise by almost 22% in 2026 to $143.5 billion, with CapEx climbing by 60.1% to $111.6 billion.

Implied Volatility Collapse

But none of this may even matter, because the stock has already run up ahead of earnings, and options positioning has become so bullish and implied volatility so high that there is a good chance that, once the stock reports, much of that could unwind, and the stock may drop sharply.

Implied volatility is elevated, with the 1-week ATM options at around 65% and likely to continue rising as earnings approach on Wednesday afternoon. But once the company releases the results, volatility typically collapses, taking both put and call premiums sharply lower, too.

The problem with the stock going higher is that the options positioning in Meta is grossly skewed bullish, which means that when call premiums decay, hedging flows and positioning are likely to unwind, negatively impacting the shares.

For example, the $675 calls expiring on May 1 have a premium of about $25, which means the stock needs to rise above $700 for those calls to be profitable. However, the current implied volatility on those calls is around 85.6%; historically, IV typically declines by 40-50% following the release of results. Even a modest 40% decline pushes the implied volatility down to about 50%, which means the premium on those calls decays from $25 to about $14.

Gamma Positioning

That means a holder of those calls is now underwater but will still need the stock to climb above $700. The first problem, though, is that the stock is in a positive gamma regime, which means that as the stock rises, market makers may very well be sellers. Additionally, $700 has a very large concentration of call gamma built up, which means that if the stock reaches $700, resistance is waiting.

Delta Positioning

Meanwhile, there is a lot of delta exposure built up at the $700 strike price as well, and once the implied volatility levels fall, the amount of delta exposure will fall too, as well as the exposure built up at higher prices, which means that market makers will most likely find themselves over-hedged in their positioning, also creating selling pressure.

This suggests that unless the stock can clear $700 post-earnings, the path of least resistance is likely lower. Because there is likely to be a lot of selling pressure released once implied volatility resets. This is not all that different from what was seen in Micron (MU) and Nvidia (NVDA) following their results.

Technical Agreement

The technical chart lays out much of this quite nicely and shows that resistance in Meta so far has been around $690, with a gap at $635 and a bigger gap around $575. It just happens to be that when looking at the gamma set-up laid out in the chart above, once the stock gets below $635, it flips to a negative regime, which means hedging flows become directional, and selling begets more selling, and that the area with the most amount of put gamma is likely not until around $550.

Unless Meta reports absolutely exceptional results and clears $700, the path of least likely resistance remains lower.

We've seen this happen before, and this time may not be any different.
